title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA)
oneLiner: Life-threatening hyperglycaemic emergency with ketoacidosis, most common in type 1 diabetes; requires rapid IV fluids, fixed-rate insulin, and aggressive potassium replacement.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Hypovolaemic shock:** Tachycardia + hypotension + reduced urine output → treat with IV fluids *before* insulin. **Key finding:** palpable weak pulse, delayed capillary refill >3 sec, systolic BP <90 mmHg.
- **Cerebral oedema (especially in children <20 years):** Altered consciousness, headache, bradycardia, hypertension, papilloedema, or asymmetric pupils. **Key finding:** sudden drop in GCS or seizure within the first 6 hours of treatment — stop insulin bolus, give IV mannitol (0.5–1 g/kg) or hypertonic saline, call senior/ICU immediately.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Polyuria, polydipsia, nocturia (days to weeks)
  - Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain (may mimic acute abdomen)
  - Fatigue, weakness, weight loss
  - Kussmaul breathing (deep, rapid breathing — respiratory compensation for acidosis)
  - Fruity (acetone) breath odour
  - Altered consciousness: drowsy, confusion, coma
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Tachycardia, hypotension, cool peripheries, dry mucous membranes (dehydration)
  - Reduced skin turgor, sunken eyes
  - Abdominal tenderness (often diffuse, no rigidity)
  - Neurological: full range from alert to obtunded
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Hyperosmolar Hyperglycaemic State (HHS):** <200 ng/L measured ketones (or negative), pH >7.3, serum osmolality >320 mOsm/kg, no Kussmaul breathing.
- **Lactic acidosis:** Normal glucose, high anion gap metabolic acidosis, elevated lactate (sepsis, ischaemia, metformin accumulation in renal failure).
- **Acute pancreatitis:** Epigastric pain radiating to back, elevated amylase/lipase, but can coexist with DKA (always check lipase).
- **Sepsis (any source):** Fever, raised CRP, localising signs (chest, urine, abdomen) — DKA can be triggered by sepsis.
- **Alcoholic ketoacidosis:** History of heavy alcohol intake, vomiting, hypoglycaemia or mild hyperglycaemia, large ketosis, minimal acidosis.
- **Starvation ketosis:** Low glucose, mild ketones, no metabolic acidosis.
- **Splitter:** Capillary ketone >3.0 mmol/L + pH <7.3 + bicarbonate <15 mmol/L = DKA (not HHS).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Capillary blood glucose (CBG) — often >15 mmol/L
  - Urine or capillary ketones (beta-hydroxybutyrate) — key to diagnosis
  - ECG: look for hyperkalaemia (tall peaked T waves) or hypokalaemia (U waves, flat T)
  - Consider venous blood gas (VBG) for pH, bicarbonate, lactate, K+
- **Bloods (urgent):**
  - Venous pH, pCO2, bicarbonate (on VBG or ABG)
  - Serum ketones (beta-hydroxybutyrate) — gold standard
  - Full blood count (WBC raised even without infection due to stress)
  - U&Es (Na, K, urea, creatinine), corrected sodium (for hyperglycaemic hyponatraemia)
  - Blood culture if fever or signs of infection
  - Amylase/lipase – to exclude pancreatitis
  - Lactate – to exclude lactic acidosis
  - HbA1c if new diagnosis
- **Imaging:**
  - Chest X-ray if respiratory symptoms or fever to rule out pneumonia
  - Consider CT head if GCS <15 or focal neurology (before lumbar puncture)
  - Abdominal imaging only if strongly suspected pancreatitis or surgical abdomen
- **Diagnostic criteria (Joint British Diabetes Societies - JBDS):**
  - Hyperglycaemia (blood glucose >11 mmol/L)
  - Ketonaemia (capillary/venous beta-hydroxybutyrate >3.0 mmol/L)
  - Metabolic acidosis (pH <7.3, bicarbonate <15 mmol/L)
- **Never-miss test: ECG** — detect silent STEMI or dangerous electrolyte abnormalities.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Fluid resuscitation:** 0.9% sodium chloride (NaCl) IV
    - First litre over 15-30 min if shocked; if not, over 60 min
    - Continue with 1 L every 1-2 hours depending on clinical response (aim for systolic BP >100 mmHg)
  - **Potassium:** Check K+ before starting insulin. If K+ <3.5 mmol/L, replace urgently before insulin (give 40 mmol KCl in 1 L 0.9% NaCl over 1 hour). If K+ 3.5–5.5 mmol/L, add 40 mmol KCl per litre of IV fluid. If K+ >5.5 mmol/L, do not add KCl initially.
  - **Insulin:** Once K+ ≥3.5 mmol/L, start fixed-rate intravenous insulin infusion (FRIII) at 0.1 units/kg/hour (rounded to nearest unit) using a 50-unit/mL syringe pump (e.g., Actrapid/Humulin S). **Do not give IV insulin bolus.**
  - **Identify and treat trigger:** Sepsis, MI, missed insulin, steroids, surgery, etc. Give broad-spectrum antibiotics if suspected infection (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam or according to local policy).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue 0.9% NaCl at 1 L every 6-8 hours, adjusting based on fluid balance, electrolytes, and urine output.
  - Add 5% glucose (e.g., 0.9% NaCl + 5% glucose) when blood glucose falls to ~14 mmol/L, to allow continued insulin administration (retain at least 0.1 U/kg/hr) until ketoacidosis resolves.
  - Monitor:
    - Hourly capillary glucose
    - Capillary ketones (every 1-2 hours initially; target decrease >0.5 mmol/L/hr)
    - VBG for pH/bicarbonate every 2-4 hours initially
    - U&Es, especially K+, every 2-4 hours
    - ECG if K+ deranged
    - NEWS2 chart every 15-30 min initially, then hourly when stable
  - Aim for:
    - K+ maintained between 4.0-5.5 mmol/L
    - Blood glucose fall of 3-6 mmol/L per hour
    - Ketones falling by >0.5 mmol/L per hour
  - Transition to subcutaneous insulin when:
    - Ketones <0.6 mmol/L (or urine ketones negative)
    - pH >7.3, bicarbonate >18 mmol/L
    - Patient eating and drinking
    - Give first subcutaneous insulin (e.g., rapid-acting analogue) 30-60 min before stopping IV insulin
  - **Default orders:**
    - Fluids: 0.9% NaCl IV at maintenance rate after initial resuscitation; if glucose falls target, add 5% glucose-containing IV fluid.
    - VTE prophylaxis: All patients with DKA should receive LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless contraindicated (bleeding risk, platelet <50)
    - Diet/NBM status: NBM until stable and tolerating oral intake; can have clear fluids if no abdominal pain/vomiting.
    - Observation frequency: NEWS2 every 1-4 hours when stable, more frequent if unwell.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Re-establish usual insulin regimen (e.g., basal-bolus or insulin pump)
  - Review sick-day rules with patient (never stop insulin, check ketones when unwell)
  - Diabetes specialist nurse (DSN) follow-up within 2 weeks
  - Address modifiable triggers (e.g., alcohol, missed doses)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line:**
  - **0.9% sodium chloride IV:** Bolus 500-1000 mL over 15-30 min if shocked; then 1 L over 1-2 hours, then 1 L every 4-6 hours. Doses: [check local policy for infusion rates]
  - **Potassium chloride (KCl):** Add 40 mmol/L of IV fluid (except if K+ >5.5). Recheck K+ after each litre until stable.
  - **Fixed-rate intravenous insulin infusion (FRIII):** 0.1 units/kg/hour IV via syringe pump. Use 50 units in 50 mL 0.9% NaCl (1 unit/mL). Doses: [check local policy for exact preparation and rate].
  - **Soluble insulin (e.g., Actrapid, Humulin S):** only via IV infusion in DKA; never give IV bolus.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Glucose 10% IV** if hypoglycaemia occurs during treatment (rare)
  - **Bicarbonate 1.26% or 8.4%** — rarely needed; only if pH <6.9 and signs of severe acidosis despite adequate fluid and insulin; give as 50-100 mmol over 30-60 min after discussion with senior.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Avoid giving bicarbonate routinely (can worsen intracellular acidosis and cause hypokalaemia)
  - Do not use short-acting SC insulin analogues as sole therapy until transition (they have slower onset than IV)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No specific dose adjustments for insulin in renal/hepatic impairment, but dose reduction may be needed if kidney function is severely impaired (risk of hypoglycaemia). KCl supplementation doses must be guided by K+ levels.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Insulin bolus (IV push) before K+ replacement:** Causes rapid hypokalaemia, potentially fatal arrhythmias. Always check K+ ≥3.5 first.
  - **Overcorrection of hyperkalaemia with too little KCl:** Initial hyperkalaemia is often due to acidosis (shift from cells); as insulin and fluids work, K+ drops rapidly — must add KCl to every IV fluid litre (unless K+ >5.5).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Hypokalaemia (most dangerous, precipitating arrhythmia)
  - Cerebral oedema (especially <20 years, high mortality)
  - Acidosis-induced cardiac arrest
  - Venous thromboembolism (severe dehydration + immobilisation)
  - Hypoglycaemia (from over-rapid glucose fall)
  - Pulmonary oedema (excessive fluid administration, especially in elderly/renal patients)
- **Chronic:**
  - Residual cognitive deficits (after severe cerebral oedema)
  - Recurrent DKA (if no sick-day management education)
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality in DKA ~2-5% in young adults, higher in elderly or with comorbidities (e.g., sepsis, MI). Most deaths occur from complications (cerebral oedema, hypokalaemia) or the underlying trigger. Full recovery is typical with timely treatment.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check capillary ketones (beta-hydroxybutyrate) at the bedside** — it is more sensitive and correlates with pH faster than urine ketones.
- **Start a continuous IV insulin infusion only after K+ ≥3.5** — this is a life-saving sequence.
- **When blood glucose reaches ~14 mmol/L, add 5% glucose to keep insulin running** — the key to clearing ketones is *insulin*, not glucose drop. Don't stop or reduce insulin until ketosis resolves.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give IV insulin bolus** – it is not indicated in DKA and causes a dangerous potassium shift.
- **Do not stop the IV insulin infusion prematurely** – transition to subcutaneous insulin only when pH >7.3 and ketones <0.6 mmol/L. Stopping early leads to rebound acidosis.
- **Do not rely only on blood glucose** – a falling glucose does not mean ketones are clearing. Always check capillary ketones or VBG pH/bicarbonate to confirm resolution of ketoacidosis.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Vomiting persists or unable to tolerate oral fluids
  - Recurrent hyperglycaemia >15 mmol/L despite rapid-acting insulin
  - Any new symptoms of ketosis (fruity breath, rapid breathing, abdominal pain)
  - Signs of hypoglycaemia or confusion
- **Follow-up:**
  - Diabetes specialist nurse (DSN) phone call within 24-48 hours
  - GP or hospital diabetes clinic within 2 weeks
  - Newly diagnosed patients: structured education within 1 month
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU when:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or sustained hypotension despite 2 L IV fluids
  - GCS <13 or any neurological concern (cerebral oedema protocol)
  - pH <6.9 despite 1 hour of treatment
  - Serum potassium <3.0 mmol/L or >6.5 mmol/L (with ECG changes)
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L (consider sepsis/ischaemia)
  - Oliguria (urine <0.5 mL/kg/hr) suggesting acute kidney injury
- **Crash team call for:**
  - Cardiac arrest or impending arrest (significant arrhythmia, hypotension unresponsive)

rawMarkdown:
## Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA)
**One-liner:** Life-threatening hyperglycaemic emergency with ketoacidosis, most common in type 1 diabetes; requires rapid IV fluids, fixed-rate insulin, and aggressive potassium replacement.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Hypovolaemic shock:** Tachycardia + hypotension + reduced urine output → treat with IV fluids *before* insulin. **Key finding:** palpable weak pulse, delayed capillary refill >3 sec, systolic BP <90 mmHg.
- **Cerebral oedema (especially in children <20 years):** Altered consciousness, headache, bradycardia, hypertension, papilloedema, or asymmetric pupils. **Key finding:** sudden drop in GCS or seizure within the first 6 hours of treatment — stop insulin bolus, give IV mannitol (0.5–1 g/kg) or hypertonic saline, call senior/ICU immediately.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Polyuria, polydipsia, nocturia (days to weeks)
  - Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain (may mimic acute abdomen)
  - Fatigue, weakness, weight loss
  - Kussmaul breathing (deep, rapid breathing — respiratory compensation for acidosis)
  - Fruity (acetone) breath odour
  - Altered consciousness: drowsy, confusion, coma
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Tachycardia, hypotension, cool peripheries, dry mucous membranes (dehydration)
  - Reduced skin turgor, sunken eyes
  - Abdominal tenderness (often diffuse, no rigidity)
  - Neurological: full range from alert to obtunded

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Hyperosmolar Hyperglycaemic State (HHS):** <200 ng/L measured ketones (or negative), pH >7.3, serum osmolality >320 mOsm/kg, no Kussmaul breathing.
- **Lactic acidosis:** Normal glucose, high anion gap metabolic acidosis, elevated lactate (sepsis, ischaemia, metformin accumulation in renal failure).
- **Acute pancreatitis:** Epigastric pain radiating to back, elevated amylase/lipase, but can coexist with DKA (always check lipase).
- **Sepsis (any source):** Fever, raised CRP, localising signs (chest, urine, abdomen) — DKA can be triggered by sepsis.
- **Alcoholic ketoacidosis:** History of heavy alcohol intake, vomiting, hypoglycaemia or mild hyperglycaemia, large ketosis, minimal acidosis.
- **Starvation ketosis:** Low glucose, mild ketones, no metabolic acidosis.
- **Splitter:** Capillary ketone >3.0 mmol/L + pH <7.3 + bicarbonate <15 mmol/L = DKA (not HHS).

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Capillary blood glucose (CBG) — often >15 mmol/L
  - Urine or capillary ketones (beta-hydroxybutyrate) — key to diagnosis
  - ECG: look for hyperkalaemia (tall peaked T waves) or hypokalaemia (U waves, flat T)
  - Consider venous blood gas (VBG) for pH, bicarbonate, lactate, K+
- **Bloods (urgent):**
  - Venous pH, pCO2, bicarbonate (on VBG or ABG)
  - Serum ketones (beta-hydroxybutyrate) — gold standard
  - Full blood count (WBC raised even without infection due to stress)
  - U&Es (Na, K, urea, creatinine), corrected sodium (for hyperglycaemic hyponatraemia)
  - Blood culture if fever or signs of infection
  - Amylase/lipase – to exclude pancreatitis
  - Lactate – to exclude lactic acidosis
  - HbA1c if new diagnosis
- **Imaging:**
  - Chest X-ray if respiratory symptoms or fever to rule out pneumonia
  - Consider CT head if GCS <15 or focal neurology (before lumbar puncture)
  - Abdominal imaging only if strongly suspected pancreatitis or surgical abdomen
- **Diagnostic criteria (Joint British Diabetes Societies - JBDS):**
  - Hyperglycaemia (blood glucose >11 mmol/L)
  - Ketonaemia (capillary/venous beta-hydroxybutyrate >3.0 mmol/L)
  - Metabolic acidosis (pH <7.3, bicarbonate <15 mmol/L)
- **Never-miss test: ECG** — detect silent STEMI or dangerous electrolyte abnormalities.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Fluid resuscitation:** 0.9% sodium chloride (NaCl) IV
    - First litre over 15-30 min if shocked; if not, over 60 min
    - Continue with 1 L every 1-2 hours depending on clinical response (aim for systolic BP >100 mmHg)
  - **Potassium:** Check K+ before starting insulin. If K+ <3.5 mmol/L, replace urgently before insulin (give 40 mmol KCl in 1 L 0.9% NaCl over 1 hour). If K+ 3.5–5.5 mmol/L, add 40 mmol KCl per litre of IV fluid. If K+ >5.5 mmol/L, do not add KCl initially.
  - **Insulin:** Once K+ ≥3.5 mmol/L, start fixed-rate intravenous insulin infusion (FRIII) at 0.1 units/kg/hour (rounded to nearest unit) using a 50-unit/mL syringe pump (e.g., Actrapid/Humulin S). **Do not give IV insulin bolus.**
  - **Identify and treat trigger:** Sepsis, MI, missed insulin, steroids, surgery, etc. Give broad-spectrum antibiotics if suspected infection (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam or according to local policy).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue 0.9% NaCl at 1 L every 6-8 hours, adjusting based on fluid balance, electrolytes, and urine output.
  - Add 5% glucose (e.g., 0.9% NaCl + 5% glucose) when blood glucose falls to ~14 mmol/L, to allow continued insulin administration (retain at least 0.1 U/kg/hr) until ketoacidosis resolves.
  - Monitor:
    - Hourly capillary glucose
    - Capillary ketones (every 1-2 hours initially; target decrease >0.5 mmol/L/hr)
    - VBG for pH/bicarbonate every 2-4 hours initially
    - U&Es, especially K+, every 2-4 hours
    - ECG if K+ deranged
    - NEWS2 chart every 15-30 min initially, then hourly when stable
  - Aim for:
    - K+ maintained between 4.0-5.5 mmol/L
    - Blood glucose fall of 3-6 mmol/L per hour
    - Ketones falling by >0.5 mmol/L per hour
  - Transition to subcutaneous insulin when:
    - Ketones <0.6 mmol/L (or urine ketones negative)
    - pH >7.3, bicarbonate >18 mmol/L
    - Patient eating and drinking
    - Give first subcutaneous insulin (e.g., rapid-acting analogue) 30-60 min before stopping IV insulin
  - **Default orders:**
    - Fluids: 0.9% NaCl IV at maintenance rate after initial resuscitation; if glucose falls target, add 5% glucose-containing IV fluid.
    - VTE prophylaxis: All patients with DKA should receive LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless contraindicated (bleeding risk, platelet <50)
    - Diet/NBM status: NBM until stable and tolerating oral intake; can have clear fluids if no abdominal pain/vomiting.
    - Observation frequency: NEWS2 every 1-4 hours when stable, more frequent if unwell.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Re-establish usual insulin regimen (e.g., basal-bolus or insulin pump)
  - Review sick-day rules with patient (never stop insulin, check ketones when unwell)
  - Diabetes specialist nurse (DSN) follow-up within 2 weeks
  - Address modifiable triggers (e.g., alcohol, missed doses)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line:**
  - **0.9% sodium chloride IV:** Bolus 500-1000 mL over 15-30 min if shocked; then 1 L over 1-2 hours, then 1 L every 4-6 hours. Doses: [check local policy for infusion rates]
  - **Potassium chloride (KCl):** Add 40 mmol/L of IV fluid (except if K+ >5.5). Recheck K+ after each litre until stable.
  - **Fixed-rate intravenous insulin infusion (FRIII):** 0.1 units/kg/hour IV via syringe pump. Use 50 units in 50 mL 0.9% NaCl (1 unit/mL). Doses: [check local policy for exact preparation and rate].
  - **Soluble insulin (e.g., Actrapid, Humulin S):** only via IV infusion in DKA; never give IV bolus.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Glucose 10% IV** if hypoglycaemia occurs during treatment (rare)
  - **Bicarbonate 1.26% or 8.4%** — rarely needed; only if pH <6.9 and signs of severe acidosis despite adequate fluid and insulin; give as 50-100 mmol over 30-60 min after discussion with senior.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Avoid giving bicarbonate routinely (can worsen intracellular acidosis and cause hypokalaemia)
  - Do not use short-acting SC insulin analogues as sole therapy until transition (they have slower onset than IV)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No specific dose adjustments for insulin in renal/hepatic impairment, but dose reduction may be needed if kidney function is severely impaired (risk of hypoglycaemia). KCl supplementation doses must be guided by K+ levels.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Insulin bolus (IV push) before K+ replacement:** Causes rapid hypokalaemia, potentially fatal arrhythmias. Always check K+ ≥3.5 first.
  - **Overcorrection of hyperkalaemia with too little KCl:** Initial hyperkalaemia is often due to acidosis (shift from cells); as insulin and fluids work, K+ drops rapidly — must add KCl to every IV fluid litre (unless K+ >5.5).

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Hypokalaemia (most dangerous, precipitating arrhythmia)
  - Cerebral oedema (especially <20 years, high mortality)
  - Acidosis-induced cardiac arrest
  - Venous thromboembolism (severe dehydration + immobilisation)
  - Hypoglycaemia (from over-rapid glucose fall)
  - Pulmonary oedema (excessive fluid administration, especially in elderly/renal patients)
- **Chronic:**
  - Residual cognitive deficits (after severe cerebral oedema)
  - Recurrent DKA (if no sick-day management education)

### Prognosis
- Mortality in DKA ~2-5% in young adults, higher in elderly or with comorbidities (e.g., sepsis, MI). Most deaths occur from complications (cerebral oedema, hypokalaemia) or the underlying trigger. Full recovery is typical with timely treatment.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check capillary ketones (beta-hydroxybutyrate) at the bedside** — it is more sensitive and correlates with pH faster than urine ketones.
- **Start a continuous IV insulin infusion only after K+ ≥3.5** — this is a life-saving sequence.
- **When blood glucose reaches ~14 mmol/L, add 5% glucose to keep insulin running** — the key to clearing ketones is *insulin*, not glucose drop. Don't stop or reduce insulin until ketosis resolves.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give IV insulin bolus** – it is not indicated in DKA and causes a dangerous potassium shift.
- **Do not stop the IV insulin infusion prematurely** – transition to subcutaneous insulin only when pH >7.3 and ketones <0.6 mmol/L. Stopping early leads to rebound acidosis.
- **Do not rely only on blood glucose** – a falling glucose does not mean ketones are clearing. Always check capillary ketones or VBG pH/bicarbonate to confirm resolution of ketoacidosis.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Vomiting persists or unable to tolerate oral fluids
  - Recurrent hyperglycaemia >15 mmol/L despite rapid-acting insulin
  - Any new symptoms of ketosis (fruity breath, rapid breathing, abdominal pain)
  - Signs of hypoglycaemia or confusion
- **Follow-up:**
  - Diabetes specialist nurse (DSN) phone call within 24-48 hours
  - GP or hospital diabetes clinic within 2 weeks
  - Newly diagnosed patients: structured education within 1 month

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU when:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or sustained hypotension despite 2 L IV fluids
  - GCS <13 or any neurological concern (cerebral oedema protocol)
  - pH <6.9 despite 1 hour of treatment
  - Serum potassium <3.0 mmol/L or >6.5 mmol/L (with ECG changes)
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L (consider sepsis/ischaemia)
  - Oliguria (urine <0.5 mL/kg/hr) suggesting acute kidney injury
- **Crash team call for:**
  - Cardiac arrest or impending arrest (significant arrhythmia, hypotension unresponsive)
